channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: DXRacer Martian Pro Series Review - Same Martian With Some Added Benefits
item.content:encoded:
Since last year's DXRacer Martian chair released the team at DXRacer has returned with a new Pro model. Taking the design of the Martian and adding even more premium bells and whistles, I was interested to see how this would compare.

DXRacer Martian Pro Series Has Everything From the Base

To start off, the DXRacer Martian is a large gaming/racer-style chair meant to be a good support through long hours of work, play, and whatever else you want to get up to at your desk.

In my review of the base model I praised it for it's comfy form factor, with a much more open back to settle into. You don't quite feel like you're getting so violently cupped into place, it also means for people who like to shift and lean and get comfy in different ways there isn't as much of a 'wrong' way to sit in the chair.

I was also a fan of the chair's electric features; adjustable recline that can be dialed in like you're adjusting the back of your car seat, and an airbag controlled lumbar support to give you a variety of degrees of support.

One thing that I did like about the base Martian was that you could get it in the leather finish or in a woven Fabric. Interestingly for the Pro series the fabric isn't an option, a fear that I had as someone whose study tends to get quite warm, but the Martian Pro has a solution for that.

The Martian Pro Adds Heading and Cooling Options

Two of the core features that are part of the 'Pro' upgrade are that there is in-seat heating and cooling. Living in Florida, I wasn't too sure how useful the heating would be, but this January and February have been of great assistance to test this out.

Located at the right side of the seat, you can switch on the heating and cooling and select from one of three different levels. During the colder weeks I greatly appreciated the ability to add some heat, even if it only covered the seat and didn't provide any warming to the back of the chair.

Likewise, as it's already immediately gotten hotter, the seat being able to cool itself has helped a lot. This assuaged my fear of not having the woven fabric option and only having leather as a choice.

Whereas the seat getting warm really does heat you up, the cooling of the seat does far more to keep it at a fresh temperature. Not cold, but also not warm. Honestly, this is more than enough (who wants to sit on the cold?), and I'm looking forward to it carrying me through the summer.

The biggest downside to the heating and cooling is that there's an automatic switch off that occurs after about 10-15 minutes. I am sure that this is a valid safety feature to have, but when it's cold and you're trying to get work does it's not only going to be cold for 15 minutes. I'd have to keep reminding myself to turn it back on, which might just be the ultimate first-world problem.

The final Pro feature is that the back of the chair now includes a massager. This isn't a vibrating massager, but instead two rows of four firm pucks that rise and lower in sequence. Starting from your lower back they'll extend, putting pressure into your back, alternating and slowly rising up.

Even with a finely shaped chair, there's still only so much sitting in one position I can really do with comfort. To have the chair be the one to take care of easing some tension in your back is a really impressive premium. 

It's also good to note that whether you're heating, cooling, or getting a back massage, there's little to no additional noise.

Battery Life Is Suddenly a Problem

One of the most impressive aspects of the Martian in my original review was the battery life. From getting the chair to the time of review, I didn't charge it once, and now it's been eight months of use and I still never had to charge it. That is certainly not the case with the Pro Series, especially if you're using those Pro features.

During the days I was using the heater functionality frequently, I'd find the battery would run out in a single day. This is a major difference from the experience of the standard Martian chair.

It's an interesting trade-off that there are premium features, but that you'll essentially need to be tethering your desk chair to the power outlet when you're not using it to make sure you're able to get the most out of it when you DO sit down in it.

If you have an office where your chair is in the middle of the room, and not near an outlet, this might be a problem, especially if no power means you can't manually adjust the chair's back either.

Same Poor Wheels…

As I have hardwood floors, I was once again disappointed by the stock feet on the chair. These are notoriously poor at swiveling and will definitely scuff up your floors. It's minor to purchase a $25 set of rollerblade wheel chair legs, but knowing how expensive the rest of the chair is, it's one place that the design is definitely held back.

DXRacer Martian Pro Series Review | Final Thoughts

When I was reached out to for this review and saw that the DXRacer Martian Pro was the Martian that I was already very much enjoying, with additional bells and whistles, it was a no brainer to check out the chair and see how many improvements have been made.

Interestingly, especially if you don't have a good way to stay plugged in, the additional features and benefits of the Pro series are very quickly balanced against the downside of just how quickly the battery runs out when you're using the Pro features.

To get the most out of the chair, you'll have to get very used to plugging it in being a part of your routine when you step away from your desk, but if you can do that then you'll get a lot of enjoyment out of it.

The DXRacer Martian Pro was reviewed with a copy provided by DXRacer - All images were taken for this review by the reviewer over 100+ hours of use.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/technology/reviews/dxracer-martian-pro-series-review-same-martian-with-some-added-benefits
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: BAFTA Games Awards 2026 Nominees Revealed with Clair Obscur Leading the Pack
item.content:encoded:
BAFTA has revealed the nominees for this year's BAFTA Games Awards, and if you're a fan of Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, you're not the only one, judging by the nominations.

Last year saw games like Astro Bot, The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom, and Black Myth: Wukong vying for the Best Game award, but since this year is the year of Our Lord and Savior Clair Obscur, the French RPG has picked up an absolute ton of nominations this time around.

It's up for pretty much every prize in which it can be nominated, including Artistic Achievement, Debut Game, and, of course, Best Game, and multiple performances by its actors can be found in the Leading and Supporting Role awards lists as well. Blimey Charlie (Cox).

Here's everything that's up for an award from BAFTA this year.

  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Dispatch
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Indiana Jones and the Great Circle
  • Arc Raiders
  • Blue Prince

Animation

  • Battlefield 6
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Dispatch
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Hades II
  • Hollow Knight: Silksong

Artistic Achievement

  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Dispatch
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Hollow Knight: Silksong
  • South of Midnight

Audio Achievement

  • Arc Raiders
  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Dispatch
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Indiana Jones and the Great Circle

British Game

  • Atomfall
  • Citizen Sleeper 2: Starward Vector
  • Mafia: The Old Country
  • Monument Valley 3
  • Powerwash Simulator 2
  • Two Point Museum

Debut Game

  • Blue Prince
  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Consume Me
  • Despelote
  • Dispatch
  • The Midnight Walk

Evolving Game

  • Vampire Survivors
  • Warhammer 40k: Space Marine 2
  • Fallout 76
  • Helldivers 2
  • Hitman World of Assassination
  • No Man's Sky

Family

  • Donkey Kong Bananza
  • Is This Seat Taken?
  • Lego Party!
  • Mario Kart World
  • Powerwash Simulator 2
  • Two Point Museum

Game Beyond Entertainment

  • The Alters
  • And Roger
  • Citizen Sleeper 2: Starward Vector
  • Consume Me
  • Despelote
  • Stalker 2: Heart of Chornobyl

Game Design

  • Ball x Pit
  • Blue Prince
  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Hades II
  • Split Fiction

Multiplayer

  • Arc Raiders
  • Dune: Awakening
  • Elden Ring Nightreign
  • Lego Party!
  • Peak
  • Split Fiction

Music

  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Dispatch
  • Ghost of Yotei
  • Hollow Knight: Silksong
  • Indiana Jones and the Great Circle

Narrative

  • The Alters
  • Blue Prince
  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Indiana Jones and the Great Circle
  • Kingdom Come: Deliverance II

New Intellectual Property

  • The Alters
  • Arc Raiders
  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Dispatch
  • South of Midnight
  • Split Fiction

Performer in a Leading Role

  • Aaron Paul - Dispatch
  • Ben Starr - Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Erika Ishii - Ghost of Yotei
  • Jennifer English - Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Tom McKay - Kingdom Come: Deliverance II
  • Troy Baker - Indiana Jones and the Great Circle

Performer in a Supporting Role

  • Alix Wilton Regan - Lies of P: Overture
  • Charlie Cox - Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Jane Perry - Dead Take
  • Jeffrey Wright - Dispatch
  • Kirsty Rider - Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Troy Baker - Death Stranding 2: On the Beach

Technical Achievement

  • Indiana Jones and the Great Circle
  • Split Fiction
  • Arc Raiders
  • Death Stranding 2: On the Beach
  • Doom: The Dark Ages
  • Ghost of Yotei

And there you have it! In fairness, Clair Obscur: Expedition 33 isn't the only recurring name on that list; there's a lot of repeat offenders across the categories. We'll get to find out who's won on April 17th!

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
